```{r}
#| lst-cap: "Code to display the ice cream inferential summary."

inferential_results_summary <- bind_rows(
  demand_inference |>
    transmute(
      query = "Demand query",
      parameter = "pi",
      hypothesis_test = "H0: pi = 0.50 versus H1: pi > 0.50",
      estimate = pi_hat_obs,
      test_statistic = z_obs,
      p_value = p_value,
      reject_h0 = reject_h0
    ),
  time_inference |>
    transmute(
      query = "Time query",
      parameter = "mu",
      hypothesis_test = "H0: mu = 12 versus H1: mu < 12",
      estimate = mu_hat_obs,
      test_statistic = z_obs,
      p_value = p_value,
      reject_h0 = reject_h0
    )
) |>
  left_join(
    ice_cream_confidence_intervals |>
      select(query, ci_lower = lower, ci_upper = upper),
    by = "query"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    across(
      c(estimate, test_statistic, p_value, ci_lower, ci_upper),
      ~ round(.x, 4)
    )
  )

inferential_results_summary |>
  knitr::kable(
    col.names = c(
      "Query",
      "Parameter",
      "Hypothesis test",
      "Estimate",
      "Test statistic",
      "p-value",
      "Reject H0?",
      "Lower limit",
      "Upper limit"
    ),
    align = c(
      "l",
      "c",
      "l",
      rep("c", 6)
    ),
    digits = c(
      NA,
      NA,
      NA,
      4,
      4,
      4,
      NA,
      4,
      4
    ),
    row.names = FALSE
  )
```

## **`Python`**

```{python}
#| echo: true
#| message: false
#| lst-label: lst-inferential-summary-py
#| lst-cap: "Code to display the ice cream inferential summary."

inferential_results_summary = pd.DataFrame({
    "query": ["Demand query", "Time query"],
    "parameter": ["pi", "mu"],
    "hypothesis_test": [
        "H0: pi = 0.50 versus H1: pi > 0.50",
        "H0: mu = 12 versus H1: mu < 12"
    ],
    "estimate": [
        demand_inference.loc[0, "pi_hat_obs"],
        time_inference.loc[0, "mu_hat_obs"]
    ],
    "test_statistic": [
        demand_inference.loc[0, "z_obs"],
        time_inference.loc[0, "z_obs"]
    ],
    "p_value": [
        demand_inference.loc[0, "p_value"],
        time_inference.loc[0, "p_value"]
    ],
    "reject_h0": [
        demand_inference.loc[0, "reject_h0"],
        time_inference.loc[0, "reject_h0"]
    ],
    "ci_lower": ice_cream_confidence_intervals["lower"],
    "ci_upper": ice_cream_confidence_intervals["upper"]
})

inferential_results_summary_display = (
    inferential_results_summary.rename(
        columns={
            "query": "Query",
            "parameter": "Parameter",
            "hypothesis_test": "Hypothesis test",
            "estimate": "Estimate",
            "test_statistic": "Test statistic",
            "p_value": "p-value",
            "reject_h0": "Reject H0?",
            "ci_lower": "Lower limit",
            "ci_upper": "Upper limit",
        }
    ).copy()
)

for column in [
    "Estimate",
    "Test statistic",
    "p-value",
    "Lower limit",
    "Upper limit",
]:
    inferential_results_summary_display[column] = (
        inferential_results_summary_display[column].map(
            lambda value: f"{value:.4f}"
        )
    )

inferential_results_summary_display["Reject H0?"] = (
    inferential_results_summary_display[
        "Reject H0?"
    ].map(
        {
            True: "Yes",
            False: "No",
        }
    )
)

inferential_results_summary_py_html = (
    inferential_results_summary_display.to_html(
        index=False,
        border=0,
    )
)
```

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| results: asis

cat(
  "\n",
  py$inferential_results_summary_py_html,
  "\n",
  sep = ""
)
```

:::

It is important to stress that a results table is helpful, but it is not the final product. The **final product** is a **statistical story that a stakeholder can understand**. That story should state the estimate, uncertainty, hypothesis-test conclusion, and assumptions in plain language.

### Parameter Testing, Goodness-of-Fit Testing, and Model Comparison {#sec-gof-versus-parameter-testing}

The hypothesis tests in this inferential section are **parameter tests**. They ask whether a parameter is equal to a benchmark value or on one side of a benchmark. This is different from the goodness-of-fit work in @sec-goodness-of-fit, where the question was whether the fitted probability model looked adequate.

![Image by [*manfredsteger*](https://pixabay.com/users/manfredsteger-1848497/) via [*Pixabay*](https://pixabay.com/vectors/idea-visualization-line-art-3976295/).](img/idea.png){width="500"}

Note that this distinction is critical because a **statistically significant parameter test** does not automatically validate the model:

- For the **demand query**, a parameter test asks whether $\pi>0.50$. On the other hand, a goodness-of-fit check asks whether a single Bernoulli probability is an adequate description of the preference data. 
- For the **time query**, a parameter test asks whether $\mu<12$. On the other hand, a goodness-of-fit check asks whether a single Exponential distribution is adequate for the waiting-time pattern.

| **Type of test or check** | **Example null hypothesis or reference claim** | **Main question** |
|:--------------------------:|:-----------------------------------------------:|:------------------:|
| **Parameter test** | $H_0 \text{: }\pi=0.50$, $H_0 \text{: }\mu=12$, or later $H_0 \text{: }\beta_j=0$. | Is a parameter equal to, above, or below a benchmark value? |
| **Goodness-of-fit check or test** | The fitted probability model is compatible with the observed data. | Does the fitted model describe the observed patterns reasonably well? |
| **Model-comparison test** | A simpler model is sufficient compared with a more complex model. | Does a more complex model improve fit enough to justify the added complexity? |

: Comparing parameter tests, goodness-of-fit checks, and model-comparison tests. {#tbl-test-types .striped .hover}

This distinction indicated in @tbl-test-types will return throughout the regression chapters. Testing whether a coefficient is different from zero is not the same as checking whether the model is well specified. A statistically significant coefficient can appear in a poorly fitting model, and a well-fitting model can still include coefficients that are not statistically significant.

### Storytelling the Inferential Results {#sec-storytelling-inferential-results}

![Image by [*Manfred Steger*](https://pixabay.com/users/manfredsteger-1848497/) via [*Pixabay*](https://pixabay.com/vectors/pixel-cells-thread-needle-diy-sew-6230155/).](img/thread.png){width="350"}

Let us now turn the inferential results back into the stakeholder-facing inquiries from @tbl-ice-cream-inferential-inquiries. At this stage, the goal is no longer to re-derive the test statistics or confidence intervals. The goal is to communicate what the evidence says about the two practical questions:

> **Does the demand sample provide evidence that chocolate is preferred by more than half of children in the target population?**

> **Does the waiting-time sample provide evidence that the average waiting time is below the 12-minute service target?**

Because we have been reporting both `R` and `Python` computations, the storytelling below presents the `R`-based figures first, with the `Python`-based figures in brackets. The two sets of numbers may differ slightly because, as noted earlier, the two languages simulated different observed random samples even when using the same conceptual data-generating setup.

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false

r_demand_estimate <- demand_inference$pi_hat_obs[1]
r_demand_p_value <- demand_inference$p_value[1]
r_demand_reject <- demand_inference$reject_h0[1]

r_time_estimate <- time_inference$mu_hat_obs[1]
r_time_p_value <- time_inference$p_value[1]
r_time_reject <- time_inference$reject_h0[1]

r_demand_ci <- ice_cream_confidence_intervals |>
  filter(query == "Demand query")

r_time_ci <- ice_cream_confidence_intervals |>
  filter(query == "Time query")

r_demand_ci_lower <- r_demand_ci$lower[1]
r_demand_ci_upper <- r_demand_ci$upper[1]

r_time_ci_lower <- r_time_ci$lower[1]
r_time_ci_upper <- r_time_ci$upper[1]

py_demand_row <- reticulate::py_to_r(
  py$demand_inference$iloc[0L]$to_dict()
)

py_time_row <- reticulate::py_to_r(
  py$time_inference$iloc[0L]$to_dict()
)

py_ci_records <- reticulate::py_to_r(
  py$ice_cream_confidence_intervals$to_dict(orient = "records")
)

py_ci_r <- bind_rows(py_ci_records)

py_demand_estimate <- py_demand_row$pi_hat_obs
py_demand_p_value <- py_demand_row$p_value
py_demand_reject <- py_demand_row$reject_h0

py_time_estimate <- py_time_row$mu_hat_obs
py_time_p_value <- py_time_row$p_value
py_time_reject <- py_time_row$reject_h0

py_demand_ci <- py_ci_r |>
  filter(query == "Demand query")

py_time_ci <- py_ci_r |>
  filter(query == "Time query")

py_demand_ci_lower <- py_demand_ci$lower[1]
py_demand_ci_upper <- py_demand_ci$upper[1]

py_time_ci_lower <- py_time_ci$lower[1]
py_time_ci_upper <- py_time_ci$upper[1]
```

#### Demand Query

> The demand team wanted to know whether chocolate is preferred by **more than half** of children in the target population. Using the observed demand sample, we estimated the chocolate-preference probability as approximately `r round(r_demand_estimate, 2)` or `r round(100 * r_demand_estimate, 0)`% (in `Python`, it is `r round(py_demand_estimate, 2)` or `r round(100 * py_demand_estimate, 0)`%). To address the directional inquiry, we tested
> $$H_0 \text{: } \pi = 0.50 \qquad \text{versus} \qquad H_1 \text{: } \pi > 0.50.$$
>
> The one-sided Central Limit Theorem-based test gives a $p\text{-value}<0.001$ in both `R` and `Python`. Since this value is `r if (r_demand_reject) "less than or equal to" else "greater than"` the significance level $\alpha=0.05$, we `r if (r_demand_reject) "reject" else "fail to reject"` $H_0$ in the inferential analysis.
>
> Interpreted for the demand team, the observed sample `r if (r_demand_reject) "provides evidence that chocolate is preferred by more than half of children in the target population" else "does not provide enough evidence to conclude that chocolate is preferred by more than half of children in the target population"`. This conclusion is based on the Bernoulli sampling model and the Central Limit Theorem approximation used for the one-proportion test.
>
> For inventory planning, the point estimate alone is not enough. The test above addresses the majority-preference question, while the confidence interval shows how much uncertainty remains around the estimated preference probability. In the inferential analysis, the $95\%$ confidence interval for $\pi$ is approximately (`r round(r_demand_ci_lower, 2)`, `r round(r_demand_ci_upper, 2)`) (in `Python`, it is approximately between `r round(py_demand_ci_lower, 2)` and `r round(py_demand_ci_upper, 2)`). Since the interval is above the practical benchmark of 0.50, this helps the demand team conclude that the observed preference for chocolate is clearly above a simple majority.

#### Time Query

> The operations team wanted to know whether the average waiting time is below the **12-minute service target**. Using the observed waiting-time sample, we estimated the average waiting time as approximately `r round(r_time_estimate, 2)` minutes (in `Python`, it is `r round(py_time_estimate, 2)` minutes). To address the directional inquiry, we tested
>
> $$H_0 \text{: } \mu = 12 \qquad \text{versus} \qquad H_1 \text{: } \mu < 12.$$
> The one-sided Central Limit Theorem-based test gives a $p\text{-value}$ of `r round(r_time_p_value, 4)` (in `Python`, it is `r format_p_value(py_time_p_value)`). Since this value is `r if (r_time_reject) "less than or equal to" else "greater than"` the significance level $\alpha=0.05$, we `r if (r_time_reject) "reject" else "fail to reject"` $H_0$ in the `R`-based inferential analysis (in `Python`, we `r if (py_time_reject) "reject" else "fail to reject"` $H_0$).
>
> Interpreted for the operations team, the observed sample `r if (r_time_reject) "provides evidence that the average waiting time is below the 12-minute service target" else "does not provide enough evidence to conclude that the average waiting time is below the 12-minute service target"`. This conclusion is based on the Central Limit Theorem approximation used for the mean-based test, with the sample standard deviation used to estimate uncertainty in the observed average waiting time.
>
> For service planning, the point estimate alone is not enough. The test above addresses the directional service-target question, while the confidence interval shows how much uncertainty remains around the estimated average waiting time. In the inferential analysis, the $95\%$ confidence interval for $\mu$ is approximately (`r round(r_time_ci_lower, 2)`, `r round(r_time_ci_upper, 2)`) minutes (in `Python`, it is approximately between `r round(py_time_ci_lower, 2)` and `r round(py_time_ci_upper, 2)` minutes). Since the interval is below the practical benchmark of 12 minutes, this helps the operations team conclude that the observed average waiting time is comfortably below the service target.

::: {.Heads-up}
:::: {.Heads-up-header}
Heads-up on reporting frequentist inference!
::::
:::: {.Heads-up-container}
A careful inference report should include four pieces: 

1. the point estimate, 
2. an uncertainty summary, 
3. the hypothesis-test conclusion when a benchmark is relevant, and 
4. the assumptions or modelling checks that support the conclusion.

![Image by [*Manfred Steger*](https://pixabay.com/users/manfredsteger-1848497/) via [*Pixabay*](https://pixabay.com/vectors/pixel-cells-lecture-lecturer-3976299/).](img/meeting.png){width="400"}

For the ice cream case, that means reporting both the MLEs and their confidence intervals, while also stating that the hypothesis tests use CLT-based Normal approximations and rely on the fitted probability models being adequate enough for the questions at hand.
::::
:::

## Chapter Summary {#sec-chapter-2-summary}

In this chapter, we used an ice cream case study to build the statistical foundation needed for the **regression models** that follow in the cookbook. We began by translating stakeholder questions into statistical inquiries about populations, samples, random variables, parameters, and observed estimates. The **demand query** focused on a binary flavour-preference variable, modelled with a Bernoulli distribution and summarized through the chocolate-preference probability $\pi$. On the other hand, the **time query** focused on a continuous waiting-time variable, modelled with an Exponential distribution and summarized through the mean waiting time $\mu$. These examples helped connect probability distributions, expected values, variance, random sampling, and sampling distributions to concrete data science questions.

![Image by [*Manfred Steger*](https://pixabay.com/users/manfredsteger-1848497/) via [*Pixabay*](https://pixabay.com/vectors/pixel-cells-emotion-confused-6230199/).](img/aha.png){width="300"}

Then, we showed how **estimation** and **model assessment** fit into this picture. Maximum likelihood estimation gave us a principled way to estimate unknown parameters from observed samples, producing $\hat{\pi}_{\operatorname{MLE,obs}}$ for the demand query and $\hat{\mu}_{\operatorname{MLE,obs}}$ for the time query. Goodness-of-fit checks then asked whether the fitted models were adequate enough for the questions being asked. For demand, the observed-versus-expected count comparison was useful as a transparent summary but limited as a stress test because the Bernoulli probability was estimated from the same sample. For time, the histogram, fitted Exponential density, ECDF comparison, and mean-versus-standard-deviation check gave us more ways to assess whether the Exponential model was reasonable.

Finally, we used the **CLT** to move from estimation to **frequentist inference**. The CLT provided approximate sampling distributions for the sample proportion and sample mean, which allowed us to construct one-sided hypothesis tests and two-sided confidence intervals. The demand test addressed whether chocolate is preferred by more than half of children in the target population, while the time test addressed whether the average waiting time is below the 12-minute service target. Storytelling translated these inferential results into stakeholder-facing conclusions with uncertainty. Therefore, this completes the inferential loop introduced in @sec-intro: define the inquiry, identify the parameter, fit the model, check goodness of fit, use an appropriate sampling distribution or approximation, and communicate the result in a way that supports decisions.

::: {.Heads-up}
:::: {.Heads-up-header}
Major food for thought for the next chapters!
::::
:::: {.Heads-up-container}
In this chapter, the parameters were simple: one probability $\pi$ for the demand query and one mean $\mu$ for the time query. In the regression chapters that follow, the same statistical workflow will remain in place, but the parameters will become more structured. Instead of estimating a single population quantity, we will often estimate a vector of $k$ regression coefficients such as

$$
\boldsymbol{\beta}
=
(\beta_0,\beta_1,\ldots,\beta_k)^\top.
$$

This shift changes the kinds of questions we can answer. Rather than asking only whether chocolate is preferred by more than half of children, a regression model might ask how flavour preference changes by city, age group, weather, or day of the week. Rather than asking only whether the average waiting time is below 12 minutes, a regression model might ask how waiting time changes with queue length, staffing level, cart location, or time of day.

![Image by [*Manfred Steger*](https://pixabay.com/users/manfredsteger-1848497/?utm_source=link-attribution&utm_medium=referral&utm_campaign=image&utm_content=3699345) via [*Pixabay*](https://pixabay.com/vectors/pixel-cells-pixel-mindmap-3704048/).](img/mind-map.png){width="350"}

The key idea is that regression does not replace the logic of this chapter. It extends it! We will still define an inquiry, choose a probability model, estimate unknown parameters, assess goodness of fit, use sampling distributions or approximations for inference, and communicate results with uncertainty. What changes is that the parameter now describes a relationship between variables, not just a single population feature.
::::
:::

## Practice Exercises {#sec-chapter-2-practice}

![Image by [*Manfred Steger*](https://pixabay.com/users/manfredsteger-1848497/?utm_source=link-attribution&utm_medium=referral&utm_campaign=image&utm_content=3699345) via [*Pixabay*](https://pixabay.com/vectors/children-teacher-rectangular-figures-7464611/).](img/exercises.png){width="550"}

The following exercises give you a chance to practice the main **probability**, **estimation**, **model-checking**, and **inference** ideas developed in this chapter using applied settings beyond the ice cream case study. The questions are organized by topic, but the question types are intentionally mixed: multiple-choice questions build fast recognition, true/false questions test precision in statistical language, and open-ended questions ask you to build complete mathematical and interpretive arguments. Across the exercises, you will work with several **probability distributions** that reappear throughout the cookbook, including Bernoulli, Binomial, Poisson, Negative Binomial, Gamma, Weibull, Lognormal, Logistic, Normal, Beta, Zero-inflated Poisson, and Multinomial models (for further information, check @sec-distributional-mind-map). The goal is not only to compute answers, but also to connect notation, assumptions, likelihoods, sampling distributions, goodness-of-fit checks, hypothesis tests, confidence intervals, and stakeholder-facing interpretation.

### Why Probability Belongs in a Regression Cookbook

::: {.Question}
::::{.Question-header}
Question 2.1
::::
::::{.Question-container}
**Multiple Choice**

A public-health dashboard records **whether a clinic visit results in a follow-up appointment**. Let

$$
F_i =
\begin{cases}
1, & \text{if visit } i \text{ results in a follow-up appointment,}\\
0, & \text{otherwise.}
\end{cases}
$$

Suppose $F_i \sim \operatorname{Bernoulli}(\pi)$, where $\pi=\Pr(F_i=1)$. Which expression is the correct PMF using the **notation conventions** from this chapter?

**A.** $p_F(f_i \mid \pi)=\pi^{f_i}(1-\pi)^{1-f_i}$, for $f_i\in\{0,1\}$.

**B.** $p_F(f_i;\pi)=\pi^{f_i}(1-\pi)^{1-f_i}$, for $f_i\in\{0,1\}$.

**C.** $f_F(f_i;\pi)=\pi^{f_i}(1-\pi)^{1-f_i}$, for $f_i\in[0,1]$.

**D.** $p_F(f_i;\pi)=f_i^{\pi}(1-f_i)^{1-\pi}$, for $\pi\in\{0,1\}$.
::::
:::

::: {.Answer}
::::{.Answer-header}
Answer 2.1
::::
::::{.Answer-container}
<details>
<summary><strong>Click here to reveal the answer!</strong></summary>

**Correct answer:** B.

**Rationale:**

The Bernoulli PMF is

$$
p_F(f_i;\pi)=\pi^{f_i}(1-\pi)^{1-f_i},
\qquad f_i\in\{0,1\}.
$$

Option B also follows the chapter convention of using a semicolon to separate the random-variable value from the distributional parameter. Option A uses conditional notation where parameter-indexed PMF notation is intended, Option C uses density notation and the wrong support, and Option D reverses the roles of the data value and the parameter.

</details>
::::
:::

::: {.Question}
::::{.Question-header}
Question 2.2
::::
::::{.Question-container}
**True or False**

A count variable $Y$ that records **the number of software errors found in a fixed one-hour testing window** can be modelled as $Y\sim\operatorname{Poisson}(\lambda)$ only if its support is finite.
::::
:::

::: {.Answer}
::::{.Answer-header}
Answer 2.2
::::
::::{.Answer-container}
<details>
<summary><strong>Click here to reveal the answer!</strong></summary>

**Correct answer:** False.

**Rationale:**

A Poisson random variable has count support

$$
y\in\{0,1,2,\ldots\},
$$

which is countably infinite, not finite (you can check @sec-classical-poisson-distribution). The Poisson model is often used for event counts in a fixed interval of time or space, provided the modelling assumptions are reasonable for the application.

</details>
::::
:::

::: {.Question}
::::{.Question-header}
Question 2.3
::::
::::{.Question-container}
**Open-ended Question**

A software reliability team models **the number of bugs $Y$ found in a fixed testing window** as

$$
Y\sim\operatorname{Poisson}(\lambda),
$$

with PMF

$$
p_Y(y;\lambda)=\frac{\exp(-\lambda)\lambda^y}{y!},
\qquad y\in\{0,1,2,\ldots\}, \quad \lambda>0.
$$

Show that this PMF sums to one over its support. Then explain why this matters before using the model in an applied analysis.
::::
:::

::: {.Answer}
::::{.Answer-header}
Answer 2.3
::::
::::{.Answer-container}
<details>
<summary><strong>Click here to reveal the answer!</strong></summary>

**Answer:**

We need to show that

$$
\sum_{y=0}^{\infty}p_Y(y;\lambda)=1.
$$

Using the Poisson PMF,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{y=0}^{\infty}p_Y(y;\lambda)
&=
\sum_{y=0}^{\infty}\frac{\exp(-\lambda)\lambda^y}{y!}\\
&=
\exp(-\lambda)\sum_{y=0}^{\infty}\frac{\lambda^y}{y!}\\
&=
\exp(-\lambda)\exp(\lambda)\\
&=1.
\end{aligned}
$$

This matters because a PMF must assign valid probabilities over all possible outcomes. If the probabilities did not sum to one, the model would not define a valid probability distribution for the count process.

</details>
::::
:::

::: {.Question}
::::{.Question-header}
Question 2.4
::::
::::{.Question-container}
**Multiple Choice**

A manufacturing team models **the proportion of a surface covered by a coating defect** as

$$
Y\sim\operatorname{Beta}(\alpha,\beta),
$$

where $0<Y<1$, $\alpha>0$, and $\beta>0$. Which statement is most appropriate?

**A.** The Beta model is suitable because $Y$ is a binary success-or-failure variable.

**B.** The Beta model is suitable because $Y$ is a continuous variable bounded between $0$ and $1$.

**C.** The Beta model is suitable because $Y$ is a nonnegative count.

**D.** The Beta model is suitable because $Y$ is continuous and unbounded.
::::
:::

::: {.Answer}
::::{.Answer-header}
Answer 2.4
::::
::::{.Answer-container}
<details>
<summary><strong>Click here to reveal the answer!</strong></summary>

**Correct answer:** B.

**Rationale:**

The Beta distribution (see @sec-beta-distribution) is a natural model for continuous random variables **whose support lies between $0$ and $1$**. It is not a binary distribution, a count distribution, or an unbounded continuous distribution.

</details>
::::
:::

::: {.Question}
::::{.Question-header}
Question 2.5
::::
::::{.Question-container}
**Open-ended Question**

A logistics analyst models **delivery time $T$ in hours** using a Gamma distribution with shape $a>0$ and scale $b>0$:

$$
f_T(t;a,b)=\frac{1}{\Gamma(a)b^a}t^{a-1}\exp\left(-\frac{t}{b}\right),
\qquad t>0.
$$

Assume

$$
\mathbb{E}(T)=ab
\qquad
\text{and}
\qquad
\operatorname{Var}(T)=ab^2.
$$

A useful way to compare variability across positive continuous variables is the **coefficient of variation**. For a random variable with positive mean, it is defined as

$$
\operatorname{CV}(T)
=
\frac{\operatorname{SD}(T)}{\mathbb{E}(T)}.
$$

Unlike the standard deviation $\operatorname{SD}(T)$, which is measured in the same units as the variable, the coefficient of variation $\operatorname{CV}(T)$ is unitless. It measures variability **relative to the mean $\mathbb{E}(T)$**. For example, a standard deviation of $2$ hours means something different when the mean delivery time is $4$ hours than when the mean delivery time is $40$ hours.

Derive the coefficient of variation for this **Gamma model** (see @sec-gamma-distribution). Then, explain why changing the shape parameter $a$ affects relative variability.
::::
:::

::: {.Answer}
::::{.Answer-header}
Answer 2.5
::::
::::{.Answer-container}
<details>
<summary><strong>Click here to reveal the answer!</strong></summary>

**Answer:**

Since

$$
\operatorname{SD}(T)
=
\sqrt{\operatorname{Var}(T)}
=
\sqrt{ab^2}
=
b\sqrt{a},
$$

we have

$$
\operatorname{CV}(T)
=
\frac{\operatorname{SD}(T)}{\mathbb{E}(T)}
=
\frac{b\sqrt{a}}{ab}
=
\frac{1}{\sqrt{a}}.
$$

The scale parameter $b$ cancels out, so the coefficient of variation is controlled only by the shape parameter $a$. Larger values of $a$ imply

$$
\operatorname{CV}(T)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{a}}
$$

is smaller. In practical terms, this means delivery times become less variable relative to their mean.

This does not necessarily mean that the standard deviation itself is small. Since

$$
\operatorname{SD}(T)=b\sqrt{a},
$$

the absolute variability still depends on both $a$ and $b$. The coefficient of variation focuses specifically on **relative variability**, which is why it is useful when comparing systems with different average delivery times.

</details>
::::
:::

::: {.Question}
::::{.Question-header}
Question 2.6
::::
::::{.Question-container}
**True or False**

If $Y\sim\operatorname{Lognormal}(\eta,\sigma^2)$ (see @sec-lognormal-distribution), then $Y$ is continuous and positive, while $\log(Y)$ is Normal with mean $\eta$ and variance $\sigma^2$ (see @sec-normal-distribution).
::::
:::

::: {.Answer}
::::{.Answer-header}
Answer 2.6
::::
::::{.Answer-container}
<details>
<summary><strong>Click here to reveal the answer!</strong></summary>

**Correct answer:** True.

**Rationale:**

The Lognormal distribution is commonly defined through the transformation

$$
\log(Y)\sim\operatorname{Normal}(\eta,\sigma^2).
$$

Because $Y=\exp\{\log(Y)\}$, the random variable $Y$ is positive and continuous. This makes Lognormal models useful for right-skewed positive outcomes such as costs, times, and concentrations.

</details>
::::
:::

::: {.Question}
::::{.Question-header}
Question 2.7
::::
::::{.Question-container}
**Multiple Choice**

A web platform records **the number of purchases $Y$** made by a user in a month. Many users make zero purchases, and the analyst suspects that some users are structurally inactive while others follow a Poisson purchasing process. Which distribution is most directly motivated by this description?

**A.** Normal

**B.** Zero-inflated Poisson

**C.** Beta

**D.** Logistic
::::
:::

::: {.Answer}
::::{.Answer-header}
Answer 2.7
::::
::::{.Answer-container}
<details>
<summary><strong>Click here to reveal the answer!</strong></summary>

**Correct answer:** B.

**Rationale:**

A Zero-inflated Poisson model (see @sec-zero-inflated-poisson-distribution) combines two mechanisms: one that generates extra zeros and another that generates counts from a Poisson distribution. This matches the description of structurally inactive users plus active users whose purchase counts may follow a Poisson-like process.

</details>
::::
:::

::: {.Question}
::::{.Question-header}
Question 2.8
::::
::::{.Question-container}
**Open-ended Question**

A product team records **the outcome of a user session as one of three categories**: *no purchase*, *one-time purchase*, or *subscription purchase*. Let

$$
\mathbf{Y}=(Y_1,Y_2,Y_3)^{\top}
$$

be the category-count vector among $n$ **independent sessions**, with

$$
\mathbf{Y}\sim\operatorname{Multinomial}(n,\boldsymbol{\pi}),
\qquad
\boldsymbol{\pi}=(\pi_1,\pi_2,\pi_3)^\top,
$$

where

$$
\pi_1+\pi_2+\pi_3=1
$$

and $Y_k$ counts how many sessions fall in category $k$ (see @sec-multinomial-distribution).

Unlike a single Bernoulli trial, the multinomial model summarizes several session outcomes through a **count vector**. For example, if $n=5$ and

$$
\mathbf{y}=(2,2,1)^{\top},
$$

then the count vector tells us that two sessions had no purchase, two had a one-time purchase, and one had a subscription purchase. However, it does not tell us the order in which those five outcomes occurred. Different session-level sequences can lead to the same final count vector.

Write the PMF of $\mathbf{Y}$ and explain the role of the multinomial coefficient in that PMF.
::::
:::

::: {.Answer}
::::{.Answer-header}
Answer 2.8
::::
::::{.Answer-container}
<details>
<summary><strong>Click here to reveal the answer!</strong></summary>

**Answer:**

For $\mathbf{y}=(y_1,y_2,y_3)^{\top}$ such that $y_k\in\{0,1,\ldots,n\}$ and $y_1+y_2+y_3=n$, the PMF is

$$
p_{\mathbf{Y}}(\mathbf{y};\boldsymbol{\pi})
=
\frac{n!}{y_1!y_2!y_3!}
\pi_1^{y_1}\pi_2^{y_2}\pi_3^{y_3}.
$$

The multinomial coefficient

$$
\frac{n!}{y_1!y_2!y_3!}
$$

counts the number of distinct session-level sequences that produce the same category-count vector. It accounts for the fact that the counts do not record the order in which the session outcomes occurred.

</details>
::::
:::

::: {.Question}
::::{.Question-header}
Question 2.9
::::
::::{.Question-container}

**True or False**

A Weibull distribution (see @sec-weibull-distribution) can be used for positive continuous **time-to-event outcomes**, and the Exponential distribution is a special case of the Weibull distribution for a particular shape parameter.

> **Hint:** To justify your answer mathematically, write the Weibull PDF under a shape-scale parametrization and check what happens when the shape parameter is set equal to $1$.
::::
:::

::: {.Answer}
::::{.Answer-header}
Answer 2.9
::::
::::{.Answer-container}
<details>
<summary><strong>Click here to reveal the answer!</strong></summary>

**Correct answer:** True.

**Rationale:**

The Weibull distribution is commonly used for positive continuous **time-to-event outcomes**, such as failure times, survival times, delivery durations, or waiting times. Under a shape-scale parametrization, a Weibull random variable $T$ with shape $a>0$ and scale $b>0$ has PDF

$$
f_T(t;a,b)
=
\frac{a}{b}
\left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^{a-1}
\exp\left[-\left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^a\right],
\qquad t>0.
$$

Now set the shape parameter equal to $1$. Then

$$
f_T(t;1,b)
=
\frac{1}{b}
\left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^{0}
\exp\left[-\left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^1\right].
$$

Since

$$
\left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^0=1,
$$

we obtain

$$
f_T(t;1,b)
=
\frac{1}{b}
\exp\left(-\frac{t}{b}\right),
\qquad t>0.
$$

This is the PDF of an Exponential distribution with mean/scale parameter $b$. Therefore, the Exponential distribution is a special case of the Weibull distribution when the Weibull shape parameter is

$$
a=1.
$$

This relationship is helpful because the Weibull distribution extends the Exponential model by allowing different hazard shapes, while the Exponential model corresponds to the simpler special case with a constant hazard rate.

</details>
::::
:::

::: {.Question}
::::{.Question-header}
Question 2.10
::::
::::{.Question-container}
**Multiple Choice**

Let $Y\sim\operatorname{Negative Binomial}(k,\pi)$ (see @sec-negative-binomial-distribution) denote the number of failures before observing $k$ successes, where $k\in\mathbb{N}$ and $0<\pi<1$. Which support is appropriate?

**A.** $y\in\{0,1,\ldots,k\}$

**B.** $y\in\{k,k+1,k+2,\ldots\}$

**C.** $y\in\{0,1,2,\ldots\}$

**D.** $0<y<1$
::::
:::

::: {.Answer}
::::{.Answer-header}
Answer 2.10
::::
::::{.Answer-container}
<details>
<summary><strong>Click here to reveal the answer!</strong></summary>

**Correct answer:** C.

**Rationale:**

If $Y$ counts the number of failures before observing $k$ successes, then zero failures are possible, one failure is possible, and there is no fixed finite upper bound. Therefore,

$$
y\in\{0,1,2,\ldots\}.
$$

</details>
::::
:::

::: {.Question}
::::{.Question-header}
Question 2.11
::::
::::{.Question-container}
**Open-ended Question**

A quality-control analyst models a continuous measurement error $Y$ using a Normal distribution with mean $m\in\mathbb{R}$ and standard deviation $s>0$ (see @sec-normal-distribution):

$$
Y\sim \operatorname{Normal}(m,s^2).
$$

The CDF can be written as

$$
F_Y(y;m,s)
=
\Phi\left(\frac{y-m}{s}\right),
\qquad y\in\mathbb{R},
$$

where $\Phi(\cdot)$ is the CDF of a standard Normal random variable. Show that the median of $Y$ is $m$.
::::
:::

::: {.Answer}
::::{.Answer-header}
Answer 2.11
::::

::::{.Answer-container}
<details>
<summary><strong>Click here to reveal the answer!</strong></summary>

**Answer:**

A median $q_{0.5}$ satisfies

$$
F_Y(q_{0.5};m,s)=0.5.
$$

Using the Normal CDF,

$$
F_Y(q_{0.5};m,s)
=
\Phi\left(\frac{q_{0.5}-m}{s}\right).
$$

We know that the standard Normal distribution is symmetric around $0$, so

$$
\Phi(0)=0.5.
$$

Therefore, to make

$$
\Phi\left(\frac{q_{0.5}-m}{s}\right)=0.5,
$$

we need

$$
\frac{q_{0.5}-m}{s}=0.
$$

Since $s>0$, this implies

$$
q_{0.5}-m=0,
$$

and hence

$$
q_{0.5}=m.
$$

Therefore, the median of $Y$ is $m$. In this Normal model, the mean, median, and centre of symmetry are all equal to $m$.

</details>
::::
:::

::: {.Question}
::::{.Question-header}
Question 2.12
::::
::::{.Question-container}
**True or False**

For a continuous random variable $Y$, the PDF value $f_Y(y;\theta)$ is itself a probability, so it must always lie between $0$ and $1$.
::::
:::

::: {.Answer}
::::{.Answer-header}
Answer 2.12
::::
::::{.Answer-container}
<details>
<summary><strong>Click here to reveal the answer!</strong></summary>

**Correct answer:** False.

**Rationale:**

For a continuous random variable, probabilities are obtained by integrating the PDF over intervals:

$$
\Pr(a\leq Y\leq b)=\int_a^b f_Y(y;\theta)\,\mathrm{d}y.
$$

The density value $f_Y(y;\theta)$ is not itself a probability and can exceed $1$, as long as the total area under the density over the support is $1$.

</details>
::::
:::

### Random Sampling and Estimation

::: {.Question}
::::{.Question-header}
Question 2.13
::::
::::{.Question-container}
**Open-ended Question**

A transportation analyst records **the number of passenger arrivals per minute at a station**. Assume

$$
Y_1,\ldots,Y_n\overset{\operatorname{i.i.d.}}{\sim}\operatorname{Poisson}(\lambda).
$$

Let

$$
\bar{Y}=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^nY_i.
$$

Derive $\mathbb{E}(\bar{Y})$ and $\operatorname{Var}(\bar{Y})$. Then, interpret both results in context.
::::
:::

::: {.Answer}
::::{.Answer-header}
Answer 2.13
::::
::::{.Answer-container}
<details>
<summary><strong>Click here to reveal the answer!</strong></summary>

**Answer:**

Since $Y_i\sim\operatorname{Poisson}(\lambda)$ (see @sec-classical-poisson-distribution),

$$
\mathbb{E}(Y_i)=\lambda,
\qquad
\operatorname{Var}(Y_i)=\lambda.
$$

By linearity of expectation,

$$
\mathbb{E}(\bar{Y})
=
\mathbb{E}\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^nY_i\right)
=
\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n\mathbb{E}(Y_i)
=
\lambda.
$$

Using independence,

$$
\operatorname{Var}(\bar{Y})
=
\operatorname{Var}\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^nY_i\right)
=
\frac{1}{n^2}\sum_{i=1}^n\operatorname{Var}(Y_i)
=
\frac{\lambda}{n}.
$$

Thus, the sample mean is unbiased for the arrival rate $\lambda$, and its variance decreases as the number of observed minutes increases.

</details>
::::
:::

::: {.Question}
::::{.Question-header}
Question 2.14
::::
::::{.Question-container}
**Multiple Choice**

A data scientist observes $Y_1,\ldots,Y_n\overset{\operatorname{i.i.d.}}{\sim}\operatorname{Bernoulli}(\pi)$ and uses

$$
\hat{\pi}=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^nY_i.
$$

Which statement is correct?

**A.** $\hat{\pi}$ is a parameter because it is unknown before data collection.

**B.** $\hat{\pi}$ is an estimator before the sample is observed and an estimate after the data are observed.

**C.** $\hat{\pi}$ is the same as $\pi$ for every possible random sample.

**D.** $\hat{\pi}$ is not random because it is computed by a formula.
::::
:::

::: {.Answer}
::::{.Answer-header}
Answer 2.14
::::
::::{.Answer-container}
<details>
<summary><strong>Click here to reveal the answer!</strong></summary>

**Correct answer:** B.

**Rationale:**

Before data are observed, $\hat{\pi}$ is a random variable because it is a function of the random sample. After the observed data are plugged in, it becomes an observed estimate. The parameter $\pi$ is fixed but unknown in the frequentist framework.

</details>
::::
:::

::: {.Question}
::::{.Question-header}
Question 2.15
::::
::::{.Question-container}
**True or False**

If $Y_1,\ldots,Y_n$ are independent and identically distributed, then their joint PMF or PDF factors into the product of the marginal PMFs or PDFs.
::::
:::

::: {.Answer}
::::{.Answer-header}
Answer 2.15
::::
::::{.Answer-container}
<details>
<summary><strong>Click here to reveal the answer!</strong></summary>

**Correct answer:** True.

**Rationale:**

For an i.i.d. sample, independence gives the product factorization and identical distribution means the same PMF or PDF is used for each observation. Thus, for a generic parameter $\theta$,

$$
p_{\mathbf{Y}}(\mathbf{y};\theta)=\prod_{i=1}^n p_Y(y_i;\theta)
$$

in the discrete case, with the analogous expression using $f_Y(y_i;\theta)$ in the continuous case.

</details>
::::
:::

::: {.Question}
::::{.Question-header}
Question 2.16
::::
::::{.Question-container}
**Open-ended Question**

A reliability engineer observes lifetimes

$$
T_1,\ldots,T_n\overset{\operatorname{i.i.d.}}{\sim}\operatorname{Exponential}(\mu),
$$

where $\mu>0$ is the mean/scale parameter. Assume

$$
\mathbb{E}(T_i)=\mu,
\qquad
\operatorname{Var}(T_i)=\mu^2.
$$

Let $\hat{\mu}=\bar{T}$. Derive $\mathbb{E}(\hat{\mu})$, $\operatorname{Var}(\hat{\mu})$, and the approximate sampling distribution of $\hat{\mu}$ for large $n$.
::::
:::

::: {.Answer}
::::{.Answer-header}
Answer 2.16
::::
::::{.Answer-container}
<details>
<summary><strong>Click here to reveal the answer!</strong></summary>

**Answer:**

Since $\hat{\mu}=\bar{T}$,

$$
\mathbb{E}(\hat{\mu})
=
\mathbb{E}(\bar{T})
=
\mu.
$$

Also, by independence,

$$
\operatorname{Var}(\hat{\mu})
=
\operatorname{Var}(\bar{T})
=
\frac{\mu^2}{n}.
$$

By the CLT,

$$
\frac{\hat{\mu}-\mu}{\mu/\sqrt{n}}
\ \dot{\sim}\
\operatorname{Normal}(0,1),
$$

or equivalently,

$$
\hat{\mu}\ \dot{\sim}\ \operatorname{Normal}\left(\mu,\frac{\mu^2}{n}\right)
$$

for large $n$.

</details>
::::
:::

::: {.Question}
::::{.Question-header}
Question 2.17
::::
::::{.Question-container}
**Multiple Choice**

A study records **the number of support tickets submitted by each customer in a month**. A natural first model for this type of count outcome is a Poisson distribution:

$$
Y_i \sim \operatorname{Poisson}(\lambda),
$$

where $Y_i$ is the number of tickets submitted by customer $i$. Under the classical Poisson model, the mean and variance are equal:

$$
\mathbb{E}(Y_i)=\lambda
\qquad
\text{and}
\qquad
\operatorname{Var}(Y_i)=\lambda.
$$

**Overdispersion** occurs when the observed count outcome varies more than the Poisson model would suggest. In practice, this often means that the sample variance is noticeably larger than the sample mean. This can happen when customers differ substantially in their underlying ticket-submission rates, when there are unobserved groups of customers, or when some customers submit many more tickets than a simple Poisson model expects.

Which comparison is most directly relevant as a first descriptive check for overdispersion?

**A.** Whether the sample median is larger than the sample maximum.

**B.** Whether the sample variance is much larger than the sample mean.

**C.** Whether the sample mean is exactly zero.

**D.** Whether the sample size is odd or even.
::::
:::

::: {.Answer}
::::{.Answer-header}
Answer 2.17
::::
::::{.Answer-container}
<details>
<summary><strong>Click here to reveal the answer!</strong></summary>

**Correct answer:** B.

**Rationale:**

For a classical Poisson model,

$$
\mathbb{E}(Y)=\operatorname{Var}(Y)=\lambda.
$$

This equality is one of the strongest modelling restrictions of the Poisson distribution. Therefore, if the sample variance is much larger than the sample mean, the observed counts are more spread out than the classical Poisson model would lead us to expect.

For example, suppose the sample mean number of monthly support tickets is close to

$$
\bar{y}=2.1,
$$

but the sample variance is

$$
s_y^2=9.4.
$$

The variance is more than four times the mean, which would be difficult to reconcile with a simple Poisson model unless the sample size is very small or the pattern is due to random sampling variation.

This descriptive check does not prove that any particular alternative model is correct. However, it is a **warning sign** that the Poisson mean-variance equality may be too restrictive. In later regression chapters, this motivates considering count models such as Negative Binomial, Generalized Poisson, or Zero-inflated Poisson.
</details>
::::
:::

::: {.Question}
::::{.Question-header}
Question 2.18
::::
::::{.Question-container}
**True or False**

Suppose $Y_1,\ldots,Y_n$ are **i.i.d. observations** from a population with finite mean $\mu$ and finite variance $\sigma^2$. The CLT says that, for a sufficiently large sample size $n$, the sampling distribution of the sample mean $\bar{Y}$ can often be approximated by

$$
\bar{Y}
\ \dot{\sim}\
\operatorname{Normal}\left(\mu,\frac{\sigma^2}{n}\right).
$$

> *True or false:* This approximation means that the original population distribution of $Y_i$ must also be Normal.
::::
:::

::: {.Answer}
::::{.Answer-header}
Answer 2.18
::::

::::{.Answer-container}
<details>
<summary><strong>Click here to reveal the answer!</strong></summary>

**Correct answer:** False.

**Rationale:**

The CLT is about the **sampling distribution of the sample mean**, not necessarily about the shape of the original population distribution. Under suitable conditions, if

$$
Y_1,\ldots,Y_n
\overset{\operatorname{i.i.d.}}{\sim}
\text{some distribution with mean } \mu \text{ and variance } \sigma^2,
$$

then the standardized sample mean satisfies the approximate relationship

$$
\frac{\bar{Y}-\mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}
\ \dot{\sim}\
\operatorname{Normal}(0,1)
$$

for sufficiently large $n$. Equivalently,

$$
\bar{Y}
\ \dot{\sim}\
\operatorname{Normal}\left(\mu,\frac{\sigma^2}{n}\right).
$$

This does **not** require the original observations $Y_i$ to be normally distributed. They could come from many different distributions, such as a Gamma, Exponential, Bernoulli, Poisson, or another distribution with finite variance. What becomes approximately Normal is the sampling distribution of $\bar{Y}$, not the original data distribution itself.

This is why the CLT is so useful for estimation and inference: it lets us use approximate Normal sampling distributions for estimators such as sample means and sample proportions, even when the original data are not Normal.

</details>
::::
:::

::: {.Question}
::::{.Question-header}
Question 2.19
::::
::::{.Question-container}
**Open-ended Question**

A platform records whether each visitor activates a new feature. Let

$$
Y_1,\ldots,Y_n\overset{\operatorname{i.i.d.}}{\sim}\operatorname{Bernoulli}(\pi),
\qquad
\hat{\pi}=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^nY_i.
$$

In estimation, the **bias** of an estimator measures whether the estimator is systematically too high or too low on average. For an estimator $\hat{\theta}$ of a parameter $\theta$, the bias is

$$
\operatorname{Bias}(\hat{\theta})
=
\mathbb{E}(\hat{\theta})-\theta.
$$

The **mean squared error (MSE)** measures the expected squared distance between the estimator and the parameter:

$$
\operatorname{MSE}(\hat{\theta})
=
\mathbb{E}\left[(\hat{\theta}-\theta)^2\right].
$$

A useful identity is

$$
\operatorname{MSE}(\hat{\theta})
=
\operatorname{Var}(\hat{\theta})
+
\left[\operatorname{Bias}(\hat{\theta})\right]^2.
$$

Derive $\operatorname{Bias}(\hat{\pi})$ and $\operatorname{MSE}(\hat{\pi})$.
::::
:::

::: {.Answer}
::::{.Answer-header}
Answer 2.19
::::
::::{.Answer-container}
<details>
<summary><strong>Click here to reveal the answer!</strong></summary>

**Answer:**

First, compute the expected value of $\hat{\pi}$:

$$
\mathbb{E}(\hat{\pi})
=
\mathbb{E}\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^nY_i\right).
$$

By linearity of expectation,

$$
\mathbb{E}(\hat{\pi})
=
\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n\mathbb{E}(Y_i).
$$

Since $Y_i\sim\operatorname{Bernoulli}(\pi)$,

$$
\mathbb{E}(Y_i)=\pi.
$$

Therefore,

$$
\mathbb{E}(\hat{\pi})
=
\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n\pi
=
\frac{n\pi}{n}
=
\pi.
$$

The bias is

$$
\operatorname{Bias}(\hat{\pi})
=
\mathbb{E}(\hat{\pi})-\pi
=
\pi-\pi
=
0.
$$

Thus, $\hat{\pi}$ is an unbiased estimator of $\pi$.

Now compute the variance of $\hat{\pi}$. Since

$$
\hat{\pi}
=
\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^nY_i,
$$

we have

$$
\operatorname{Var}(\hat{\pi})
=
\operatorname{Var}\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^nY_i\right).
$$

Using independence,

$$
\operatorname{Var}(\hat{\pi})
=
\frac{1}{n^2}\sum_{i=1}^n\operatorname{Var}(Y_i).
$$

Since $Y_i\sim\operatorname{Bernoulli}(\pi)$,

$$
\operatorname{Var}(Y_i)=\pi(1-\pi).
$$

Therefore,

$$
\operatorname{Var}(\hat{\pi})
=
\frac{1}{n^2}\sum_{i=1}^n\pi(1-\pi)
=
\frac{n\pi(1-\pi)}{n^2}
=
\frac{\pi(1-\pi)}{n}.
$$

Using the bias-variance decomposition,

$$
\operatorname{MSE}(\hat{\pi})
=
\operatorname{Var}(\hat{\pi})
+
\left[\operatorname{Bias}(\hat{\pi})\right]^2.
$$

Since $\operatorname{Bias}(\hat{\pi})=0$,

$$
\operatorname{MSE}(\hat{\pi})
=
\frac{\pi(1-\pi)}{n}
+
0^2
=
\frac{\pi(1-\pi)}{n}.
$$

So,

$$
\boxed{
\operatorname{Bias}(\hat{\pi})=0
}
\qquad
\text{and}
\qquad
\boxed{
\operatorname{MSE}(\hat{\pi})=\frac{\pi(1-\pi)}{n}.
}
$$

The result shows that the estimator is centred correctly on $\pi$, and its mean squared error decreases as the sample size $n$ increases.
</details>
::::
:::

::: {.Question}
::::{.Question-header}
Question 2.20
::::
::::{.Question-container}
**Multiple Choice**

A researcher observes $Y_1,\ldots,Y_n\overset{\operatorname{i.i.d.}}{\sim}\operatorname{Gamma}(a,b)$, where $a$ and $b$ are fixed unknown parameters (see @sec-gamma-distribution). Which statement best describes the observed sample $\mathbf{y}=(y_1,\ldots,y_n)^{\top}$ after data collection?

**A.** $\mathbf{y}$ is random because it came from a random process.

**B.** $\mathbf{y}$ is fixed once observed, while the estimator before observation is random.

**C.** $\mathbf{y}$ is the same as the parameter vector.

**D.** $\mathbf{y}$ is a probability distribution.
::::
:::

::: {.Answer}
::::{.Answer-header}
Answer 2.20
::::
::::{.Answer-container}
<details>
<summary><strong>Click here to reveal the answer!</strong></summary>

**Correct answer:** B.

**Rationale:**

Before data collection, the sample is represented by random variables. After data collection, the realized values are fixed observed data. Estimators are functions of random variables before observation and become estimates after the observed data are plugged in.

</details>
::::
:::

::: {.Question}
::::{.Question-header}
Question 2.21
::::
::::{.Question-container}
**True or False**

The sample mean of i.i.d. Lognormal observations (see @sec-lognormal-distribution) is guaranteed to have an exactly Normal (see @sec-normal-distribution) sampling distribution for every sample size $n$.
::::
:::

::: {.Answer}
::::{.Answer-header}
Answer 2.21
::::
::::{.Answer-container}
<details>
<summary><strong>Click here to reveal the answer!</strong></summary>

**Correct answer:** False.

**Rationale:**

Lognormal observations are not Normal on the original scale. The CLT may justify an approximate Normal sampling distribution for the sample mean when the sample size $n$ is sufficiently large and the variance is finite, but the distribution **is not exactly Normal** for every sample size.

</details>
::::
:::

::: {.Question}
::::{.Question-header}
Question 2.22
::::
::::{.Question-container}
**Open-ended Question**

A biomedical researcher records $Y_1,\ldots,Y_n\overset{\operatorname{i.i.d.}}{\sim}\operatorname{Beta}(\alpha,\beta)$ (see @sec-beta-distribution), where

$$
\mathbb{E}(Y_i)=\frac{\alpha}{\alpha+\beta},
\qquad
\operatorname{Var}(Y_i)=\frac{\alpha\beta}{(\alpha+\beta)^2(\alpha+\beta+1)}.
$$

Let $\bar{Y}=n^{-1}\sum_{i=1}^nY_i$. Derive the large-sample approximate distribution of $\bar{Y}$.
::::
:::

::: {.Answer}
::::{.Answer-header}
Answer 2.22
::::
::::{.Answer-container}
<details>
<summary><strong>Click here to reveal the answer!</strong></summary>

**Answer:**

By the CLT,

$$
\frac{\bar{Y}-\mathbb{E}(Y_i)}{\sqrt{\operatorname{Var}(Y_i)/n}}
\ \dot{\sim}\
\operatorname{Normal}(0,1).
$$

Substituting the Beta mean and variance gives

$$
\bar{Y}
\ \dot{\sim}\
\operatorname{Normal}\left(
\frac{\alpha}{\alpha+\beta},
\frac{1}{n}\frac{\alpha\beta}{(\alpha+\beta)^2(\alpha+\beta+1)}
\right).
$$

This approximation becomes more useful as $n$ increases, provided the sample is reasonably treated as i.i.d. from the Beta model.

</details>
::::
:::

::: {.Question}
::::{.Question-header}
Question 2.23
::::
::::{.Question-container}
**Multiple Choice**

Suppose $Y_1,\ldots,Y_n$ are i.i.d. with finite mean $\mu$ and finite variance $\sigma^2$. Which expression is the CLT-based pivot for the sample mean when $\sigma$ is known?

**A.** $\displaystyle \frac{\bar{Y}-\mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}\ \dot{\sim}\ \operatorname{Normal}(0,1)$

**B.** $\displaystyle \frac{\bar{Y}-\sigma}{\mu/\sqrt{n}}\ \dot{\sim}\ \operatorname{Normal}(0,1)$

**C.** $\displaystyle \frac{Y_i-\bar{Y}}{n}\ \dot{\sim}\ \operatorname{Normal}(0,1)$

**D.** $\displaystyle \frac{\mu-\sigma}{\bar{Y}}\ \dot{\sim}\ \operatorname{Normal}(0,1)$
::::
:::

::: {.Answer}
::::{.Answer-header}
Answer 2.23
::::
::::{.Answer-container}
<details>
<summary><strong>Click here to reveal the answer!</strong></summary>

**Correct answer:** A.

**Rationale:**

The CLT standardizes the sample mean by subtracting its mean and dividing by its standard error:

$$
\frac{\bar{Y}-\mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}
\ \dot{\sim}\
\operatorname{Normal}(0,1).
$$

The denominator is the standard deviation of the sample mean, not the standard deviation of a single observation.

</details>
::::
:::

::: {.Question}
::::{.Question-header}
Question 2.24
::::
::::{.Question-container}
**True or False**

An estimator can be biased and still have a smaller MSE than an unbiased estimator.
::::
:::

::: {.Answer}
::::{.Answer-header}
Answer 2.24
::::
::::{.Answer-container}
<details>
<summary><strong>Click here to reveal the answer!</strong></summary>

**Correct answer:** True.

**Rationale:**

MSE decomposes as

$$
\operatorname{MSE}(\hat{\theta})
=
\operatorname{Var}(\hat{\theta})+ [\operatorname{Bias}(\hat{\theta})\bigr]^2.
$$

A biased estimator can have lower variance by enough to offset its squared bias, yielding smaller MSE than an unbiased estimator. This is a central bias-variance tradeoff idea.

</details>
::::
:::

### Maximum Likelihood Estimation

::: {.Question}
::::{.Question-header}
Question 2.25
::::
::::{.Question-container}
**Open-ended Question**

A quality team observes $Y_1,\ldots,Y_n\overset{\operatorname{i.i.d.}}{\sim}\operatorname{Poisson}(\lambda)$ (see @sec-classical-poisson-distribution). Derive the maximum likelihood estimator of $\lambda$.
::::
:::

::: {.Answer}
::::{.Answer-header}
Answer 2.25
::::
::::{.Answer-container}
<details>
<summary><strong>Click here to reveal the answer!</strong></summary>

**Answer:**

With $\mathbf{y}=(y_1,\ldots,y_n)^{\top}$, the likelihood is

$$
\mathcal{L}(\lambda;\mathbf{y})
=
\prod_{i=1}^n\frac{\exp(-\lambda)\lambda^{y_i}}{y_i!}
=
\exp(-n\lambda)\lambda^{\sum_{i=1}^ny_i}\prod_{i=1}^n\frac{1}{y_i!}.
$$

The log-likelihood is

$$
\ell(\lambda;\mathbf{y})
=
-n\lambda+\left(\sum_{i=1}^ny_i\right)\log(\lambda)-\sum_{i=1}^n\log(y_i!).
$$

Differentiate:

$$
\frac{\partial \ell(\lambda;\mathbf{y})}{\partial \lambda}
=
-n+\frac{\sum_{i=1}^ny_i}{\lambda}.
$$

Set equal to zero:

$$
-n+\frac{\sum_{i=1}^ny_i}{\lambda}=0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\hat{\lambda}_{\operatorname{MLE}}
=
\bar{Y}.
$$

The second derivative is

$$
\frac{\partial^2 \ell(\lambda;\mathbf{y})}{\partial \lambda^2}
=
-\frac{\sum_{i=1}^ny_i}{\lambda^2}<0
$$

whenever at least one event is observed, confirming a maximum.

</details>
::::
:::

::: {.Question}
::::{.Question-header}
Question 2.26
::::
::::{.Question-container}
**Multiple Choice**

A researcher observes $Y_1,\ldots,Y_n\overset{\operatorname{i.i.d.}}{\sim}\operatorname{Bernoulli}(\pi)$. With $\mathbf{y}=(y_1,\ldots,y_n)^{\top}$, which likelihood is correct?

**A.** $\mathcal{L}(\pi;\mathbf{y})=\prod_{i=1}^n\pi^{y_i}(1-\pi)^{1-y_i}$

**B.** $\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{y};\pi)=\prod_{i=1}^n\pi^{\pi}(1-y_i)^{1-\pi}$

**C.** $\mathcal{L}(\pi;\mathbf{y})=\sum_{i=1}^n\pi^{y_i}(1-\pi)^{1-y_i}$

**D.** $\mathcal{L}(\pi;\mathbf{y})=\pi(1-\pi)$
::::
:::

::: {.Answer}
::::{.Answer-header}
Answer 2.26
::::
::::{.Answer-container}
<details>
<summary><strong>Click here to reveal the answer!</strong></summary>

**Correct answer:** A.

**Rationale:**

For i.i.d. Bernoulli observations, the likelihood is the product of the PMFs evaluated at the observed data:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\pi;\mathbf{y})
=
\prod_{i=1}^n\pi^{y_i}(1-\pi)^{1-y_i}.
$$

Option A also follows the chapter convention of writing the parameter before the semicolon and the observed data after it in likelihood notation.

</details>
::::
:::

::: {.Question}
::::{.Question-header}
Question 2.27
::::
::::{.Question-container}
**True or False**

In frequentist maximum likelihood estimation, it is more precise to say that the MLE is the parameter value most compatible with the observed data under the model, rather than saying the parameter value is the most probable.
::::
:::

::: {.Answer}
::::{.Answer-header}
Answer 2.27
::::
::::{.Answer-container}
<details>
<summary><strong>Click here to reveal the answer!</strong></summary>

**Correct answer:** True.

**Rationale:**

In the frequentist framework, **the parameter is fixed but unknown**. The likelihood is a function of the parameter after the data are observed, but it is not a probability distribution over the parameter. Therefore, saying that the MLE is most compatible with the observed data under the model is more precise.

</details>
::::
:::

::: {.Question}
::::{.Question-header}
Question 2.28
::::
::::{.Question-container}
**Open-ended Question**

A risk analyst models **large insurance claim amounts** using a Pareto distribution (see @sec-pareto-distribution). Let

$$
Y_1,\ldots,Y_n
\overset{\operatorname{i.i.d.}}{\sim}
\operatorname{Pareto}(y_{\min}, a),
$$

where $y_{\min}>0$ is a known minimum claim threshold and $a>0$ is an unknown shape parameter. The PDF is

$$
f_Y(y;a)
=
\frac{a y_{\min}^{a}}{y^{a+1}},
\qquad y\geq y_{\min}.
$$

The Pareto distribution is often used for positive, heavy-tailed outcomes. In this parametrization, smaller values of $a$ correspond to heavier tails, meaning that very large claims become more plausible.

Assume $y_{\min}$ is known. Derive the MLE of the shape parameter $a$.
::::
:::

::: {.Answer}
::::{.Answer-header}
Answer 2.28
::::
::::{.Answer-container}
<details>
<summary><strong>Click here to reveal the answer!</strong></summary>

**Answer:**

The likelihood is

$$
\mathcal{L}(a;\mathbf{y})
=
\prod_{i=1}^n
\frac{a y_{\min}^{a}}{y_i^{a+1}},
\qquad y_i\geq y_{\min}.
$$

We can separate the terms involving $a$:

$$
\mathcal{L}(a;\mathbf{y})
=
a^n
y_{\min}^{an}
\prod_{i=1}^n y_i^{-(a+1)}.
$$

Taking logs gives the log-likelihood

$$
\ell(a;\mathbf{y})
=
n\log(a)
+
an\log(y_{\min})
-
(a+1)\sum_{i=1}^n \log(y_i).
$$

Now differentiate with respect to $a$:

$$
\frac{\partial \ell(a;\mathbf{y})}{\partial a}
=
\frac{n}{a}
+
n\log(y_{\min})
-
\sum_{i=1}^n \log(y_i).
$$

Set the score equal to zero:

$$
\frac{n}{a}
+
n\log(y_{\min})
-
\sum_{i=1}^n \log(y_i)
=
0.
$$

Rearranging,

$$
\frac{n}{a}
=
\sum_{i=1}^n \log(y_i)
-
n\log(y_{\min}).
$$

Since

$$
\sum_{i=1}^n \log(y_i)
-
n\log(y_{\min})
=
\sum_{i=1}^n
\log\left(\frac{y_i}{y_{\min}}\right),
$$

we obtain

$$
\frac{n}{a}
=
\sum_{i=1}^n
\log\left(\frac{y_i}{y_{\min}}\right).
$$

Therefore, the MLE is

$$
\hat{a}_{\operatorname{MLE}}
=
\frac{n}{
\sum_{i=1}^n
\log\left(\frac{Y_i}{y_{\min}}\right)
}.
$$

To check that this critical point is a maximum, compute the second derivative:

$$
\frac{\partial^2 \ell(a;\mathbf{y})}{\partial a^2}
=
-\frac{n}{a^2}.
$$

Since $a>0$,

$$
-\frac{n}{a^2}<0,
$$

so the log-likelihood is concave in $a$ and the critical point is the maximum.

Thus,

$$
\boxed{
\hat{a}_{\operatorname{MLE}}
=
\frac{n}{
\sum_{i=1}^n
\log\left(\frac{Y_i}{y_{\min}}\right)
}.
}
$$

In practical terms, if many claims are far above $y_{\min}$, then the **observed logarithmic ratios $\log(y_i/y_{\min})$** tend to be larger, which makes $\hat{a}_{\operatorname{MLE}}$ smaller. This corresponds to a heavier fitted tail. If claims are mostly close to $y_{\min}$, then the logarithmic ratios tend to be smaller, which makes $\hat{a}_{\operatorname{MLE}}$ larger and indicates a lighter fitted tail.

</details>
::::
:::

::: {.Question}
::::{.Question-header}
Question 2.29
::::
::::{.Question-container}
**Multiple Choice**

A data scientist fits a Normal model

$$
Y_i
\overset{\operatorname{i.i.d.}}{\sim}
\operatorname{Normal}(\mu,\sigma^2),
$$

with both $\mu$ and $\sigma^2$ unknown (see @sec-normal-distribution). Which estimator is the MLE of $\sigma^2$?

**A.** $\displaystyle \hat{\sigma}^2_{\operatorname{MLE}}=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n(Y_i-\bar{Y})^2$

**B.** $\displaystyle \hat{\sigma}^2_{\operatorname{MLE}}=\frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^n(Y_i-\bar{Y})^2$

**C.** $\displaystyle \hat{\sigma}^2_{\operatorname{MLE}}=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^nY_i^2$

**D.** $\displaystyle \hat{\sigma}^2_{\operatorname{MLE}}=\bar{Y}$
::::
:::

::: {.Answer}
::::{.Answer-header}
Answer 2.29
::::

::::{.Answer-container}
<details>
<summary><strong>Click here to reveal the answer!</strong></summary>

**Correct answer:** A.

**Rationale:**

For the Normal model,

$$
Y_i
\overset{\operatorname{i.i.d.}}{\sim}
\operatorname{Normal}(\mu,\sigma^2),
$$

the PDF of one observation is

$$
f_Y(y_i;\mu,\sigma^2)
=
\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}
\exp\left[
-\frac{(y_i-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}
\right],
\qquad -\infty<y_i<\infty.
$$

For an observed sample $\mathbf{y}=(y_1,\ldots,y_n)^\top$, the likelihood is

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mu,\sigma^2;\mathbf{y})
=
\prod_{i=1}^n
\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}
\exp\left[
-\frac{(y_i-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}
\right].
$$

Combining terms,

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mu,\sigma^2;\mathbf{y})
=
(2\pi\sigma^2)^{-n/2}
\exp\left[
-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}
\sum_{i=1}^n (y_i-\mu)^2
\right].
$$

The log-likelihood is

$$
\ell(\mu,\sigma^2;\mathbf{y})
=
-\frac{n}{2}\log(2\pi)
-\frac{n}{2}\log(\sigma^2)
-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}
\sum_{i=1}^n (y_i-\mu)^2.
$$

First, maximize with respect to $\mu$. Differentiate:

$$
\frac{\partial \ell(\mu,\sigma^2;\mathbf{y})}{\partial \mu}
=
\frac{1}{\sigma^2}
\sum_{i=1}^n(y_i-\mu).
$$

Set the score equal to zero:

$$
\frac{1}{\sigma^2}
\sum_{i=1}^n(y_i-\mu)
=
0.
$$

Since $\sigma^2>0$, this is equivalent to

$$
\sum_{i=1}^n(y_i-\mu)=0.
$$

Then

$$
\sum_{i=1}^n y_i - n\mu = 0,
$$

so

$$
\hat{\mu}_{\operatorname{MLE}}
=
\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n y_i
=
\bar{y}.
$$

Now maximize with respect to $\sigma^2$. Let

$$
v=\sigma^2.
$$

Then the log-likelihood can be written as

$$
\ell(\mu,v;\mathbf{y})
=
-\frac{n}{2}\log(2\pi)
-\frac{n}{2}\log(v)
-\frac{1}{2v}
\sum_{i=1}^n (y_i-\mu)^2,
\qquad v>0.
$$

Differentiate with respect to $v$:

$$
\frac{\partial \ell(\mu,v;\mathbf{y})}{\partial v}
=
-\frac{n}{2v}
+
\frac{1}{2v^2}
\sum_{i=1}^n (y_i-\mu)^2.
$$

Set the score equal to zero:

$$
-\frac{n}{2v}
+
\frac{1}{2v^2}
\sum_{i=1}^n (y_i-\mu)^2
=
0.
$$

Multiply both sides by $2v^2$:

$$
-nv
+
\sum_{i=1}^n (y_i-\mu)^2
=
0.
$$

Therefore,

$$
v
=
\frac{1}{n}
\sum_{i=1}^n (y_i-\mu)^2.
$$

Since the MLE of $\mu$ is $\hat{\mu}_{\operatorname{MLE}}=\bar{y}$, the MLE of $\sigma^2$ is

$$
\hat{\sigma}^2_{\operatorname{MLE}}
=
\frac{1}{n}
\sum_{i=1}^n (y_i-\bar{y})^2.
$$

In random-variable notation, this corresponds to

$$
\hat{\sigma}^2_{\operatorname{MLE}}
=
\frac{1}{n}
\sum_{i=1}^n (Y_i-\bar{Y})^2.
$$

Thus, the correct answer is

$$
\boxed{
\hat{\sigma}^2_{\operatorname{MLE}}
=
\frac{1}{n}
\sum_{i=1}^n (Y_i-\bar{Y})^2.
}
$$

The estimator with denominator $n-1$,

$$
\frac{1}{n-1}
\sum_{i=1}^n (Y_i-\bar{Y})^2,
$$

is the usual unbiased sample variance. It corrects the bias introduced by estimating $\mu$ with $\bar{Y}$, but it is not the MLE. The MLE is obtained by directly maximizing the Normal likelihood, and that optimization gives denominator $n$.

</details>
::::
:::

::: {.Question}
::::{.Question-header}
Question 2.30
::::
::::{.Question-container}
**True or False**

A hospital operations team models **the number of patient arrivals to an urgent-care unit in one hour** as

$$
Y_i \overset{\operatorname{i.i.d.}}{\sim} \operatorname{Poisson}(\lambda),
$$

where $\lambda>0$ is the average number of arrivals per hour. Suppose the MLE of $\lambda$ is

$$
\hat{\lambda}_{\operatorname{MLE}}
=
\bar{Y}.
$$

The operations team is not only interested in the arrival rate $\lambda$. They also want to estimate the probability that **at least one patient arrives in a future one-hour period**. Under the Poisson model, this probability is

$$
g(\lambda)
=
\Pr(Y\geq 1)
=
1-\Pr(Y=0)
=
1-\exp(-\lambda).
$$

> *True or false:* By the invariance property of MLEs, the MLE of $g(\lambda)=1-\exp(-\lambda)$ is

$$
g(\hat{\lambda}_{\operatorname{MLE}})
=
1-\exp(-\hat{\lambda}_{\operatorname{MLE}})
=
1-\exp(-\bar{Y}).
$$
::::
:::

::: {.Answer}
::::{.Answer-header}
Answer 2.30
::::
::::{.Answer-container}
<details>
<summary><strong>Click here to reveal the answer!</strong></summary>

**Correct answer:** True.

**Rationale:**

The invariance property of MLEs says that if $\hat{\theta}_{\operatorname{MLE}}$ is the MLE of $\theta$, then, for a suitable transformation $g(\cdot)$, the MLE of $g(\theta)$ is

$$
g(\hat{\theta}_{\operatorname{MLE}}).
$$

In this problem, the original model parameter is the Poisson rate $\lambda$, and the MLE is

$$
\hat{\lambda}_{\operatorname{MLE}}
=
\bar{Y}.
$$

The stakeholder-facing quantity is not $\lambda$ itself, but the probability of at least one arrival in a future hour:

$$
g(\lambda)
=
\Pr(Y\geq 1).
$$

Using the Poisson PMF,

$$
p_Y(y;\lambda)
=
\frac{\lambda^y\exp(-\lambda)}{y!},
\qquad y=0,1,2,\ldots,
$$

we have

$$
\Pr(Y=0)
=
p_Y(0;\lambda)
=
\frac{\lambda^0\exp(-\lambda)}{0!}
=
\exp(-\lambda).
$$

Therefore,

$$
\Pr(Y\geq 1)
=
1-\Pr(Y=0)
=
1-\exp(-\lambda).
$$

Thus, the quantity of interest is

$$
g(\lambda)=1-\exp(-\lambda).
$$

By the invariance property, the MLE of this probability is obtained by replacing $\lambda$ with its MLE:

$$
\widehat{g(\lambda)}_{\operatorname{MLE}}
=
g(\hat{\lambda}_{\operatorname{MLE}})
=
1-\exp(-\hat{\lambda}_{\operatorname{MLE}}).
$$

Since

$$
\hat{\lambda}_{\operatorname{MLE}}=\bar{Y},
$$

we get

$$
\widehat{\Pr(Y\geq 1)}_{\operatorname{MLE}}
=
1-\exp(-\bar{Y}).
$$

So the statement is true. In practical terms, once the hospital estimates the average hourly arrival rate, it can use the fitted Poisson model to estimate the probability of seeing at least one arrival in a future hour.

</details>
::::
:::

::: {.Question}
::::{.Question-header}
Question 2.31
::::
::::{.Question-container}
**Open-ended Question**

A risk team observes $Y_1,\ldots,Y_n\overset{\operatorname{i.i.d.}}{\sim}\operatorname{Bernoulli}(\pi)$. Derive the MLE of the odds

$$
\omega=\frac{\pi}{1-\pi}.
$$

Assume $0<\sum_{i=1}^n y_i<n$.
::::
:::

::: {.Answer}
::::{.Answer-header}
Answer 2.31
::::
::::{.Answer-container}
<details>
<summary><strong>Click here to reveal the answer!</strong></summary>

**Answer:**

For i.i.d. Bernoulli observations, the MLE of $\pi$ is

$$
\hat{\pi}_{\operatorname{MLE}}=\bar{Y}.
$$

The odds are a transformation of $\pi$:

$$
\omega=g(\pi)=\frac{\pi}{1-\pi}.
$$

By the invariance property of MLEs,

$$
\hat{\omega}_{\operatorname{MLE}}
=
g(\hat{\pi}_{\operatorname{MLE}})
=
\frac{\hat{\pi}_{\operatorname{MLE}}}{1-\hat{\pi}_{\operatorname{MLE}}}
=
\frac{\bar{Y}}{1-\bar{Y}}.
$$

Equivalently, if $s=\sum_{i=1}^ny_i$, then

$$
\hat{\omega}_{\operatorname{MLE}}
=
\frac{s/n}{1-s/n}
=
\frac{s}{n-s}.
$$

</details>
::::
:::

::: {.Question}
::::{.Question-header}
Question 2.32
::::

::::{.Question-container}
**Multiple Choice**

A sample $y_1,\ldots,y_n$ comes from a Lognormal model (see @sec-lognormal-distribution), where

$$
\log(Y_i)\overset{\operatorname{i.i.d.}}{\sim}\operatorname{Normal}(\eta,\sigma^2).
$$

Here, $Y_i$ is positive and right-skewed on its original scale, but $\log(Y_i)$ is modelled as Normal. If $\sigma^2$ is known, which expression is the MLE of $\eta$?

**A.** $\displaystyle \hat{\eta}_{\operatorname{MLE}}=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n Y_i$

**B.** $\displaystyle \hat{\eta}_{\operatorname{MLE}}=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n \log(Y_i)$

**C.** $\displaystyle \hat{\eta}_{\operatorname{MLE}}=\log\left(\sum_{i=1}^nY_i\right)$

**D.** $\displaystyle \hat{\eta}_{\operatorname{MLE}}=\frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^n\log(Y_i)$
::::
:::

::: {.Answer}
::::{.Answer-header}
Answer 2.32
::::

::::{.Answer-container}
<details>
<summary><strong>Click here to reveal the answer!</strong></summary>

**Correct answer:** B.

**Rationale:**

The key point is that the Normal model is placed on the **log-transformed observations**, not on the original observations. Define

$$
X_i=\log(Y_i).
$$

Then the model statement

$$
\log(Y_i)\overset{\operatorname{i.i.d.}}{\sim}\operatorname{Normal}(\eta,\sigma^2)
$$

can be rewritten as

$$
X_i\overset{\operatorname{i.i.d.}}{\sim}\operatorname{Normal}(\eta,\sigma^2),
$$

where $\eta$ is the mean of the log-transformed variable $X_i$.

Since $\sigma^2$ is known, the likelihood for $\eta$ based on the transformed observations $x_i=\log(y_i)$ is proportional to

$$
\mathcal{L}(\eta;\mathbf{x})
\propto
\prod_{i=1}^n
\exp\left[
-\frac{(x_i-\eta)^2}{2\sigma^2}
\right].
$$

Taking logs and dropping constants that do not depend on $\eta$ gives

$$
\ell(\eta;\mathbf{x})
=
-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}
\sum_{i=1}^n (x_i-\eta)^2
+
\text{constant}.
$$

Thus, maximizing the log-likelihood is equivalent to minimizing

$$
\sum_{i=1}^n (x_i-\eta)^2.
$$

Differentiate the log-likelihood with respect to $\eta$:

$$
\frac{\partial \ell(\eta;\mathbf{x})}{\partial \eta}
=
\frac{1}{\sigma^2}
\sum_{i=1}^n(x_i-\eta).
$$

Set the score equal to zero:

$$
\frac{1}{\sigma^2}
\sum_{i=1}^n(x_i-\eta)
=
0.
$$

Since $\sigma^2>0$, this is equivalent to

$$
\sum_{i=1}^n x_i - n\eta = 0.
$$

Therefore,

$$
\hat{\eta}_{\operatorname{MLE}}
=
\frac{1}{n}
\sum_{i=1}^n X_i.
$$

Substituting back $X_i=\log(Y_i)$ gives

$$
\hat{\eta}_{\operatorname{MLE}}
=
\frac{1}{n}
\sum_{i=1}^n \log(Y_i).
$$

So the correct answer is **B**.

Option A averages the observations on the original scale, but $\eta$ is the mean on the log scale. Option C takes the log after summing, which is not the same as averaging log-values. Option D uses denominator $n-1$, which is not the MLE for a Normal mean.

</details>
::::
:::

::: {.Question}
::::{.Question-header}
Question 2.33
::::
::::{.Question-container}
**True or False**

In maximum likelihood estimation, the goal is to find the parameter value that maximizes the likelihood function. With $\mathbf{y}=(y_1,\ldots,y_n)^{\top}$ and parameter $\theta$, suppose two likelihood functions are related by

$$
\mathcal{L}_2(\theta;\mathbf{y})
=
c\,\mathcal{L}_1(\theta;\mathbf{y}),
$$

where $c>0$ is a constant that does **not** depend on $\theta$. 

> *True or false:* Multiplying the likelihood by this positive constant changes the location of the MLE.
::::
:::

::: {.Answer}
::::{.Answer-header}
Answer 2.33
::::

::::{.Answer-container}
<details>
<summary><strong>Click here to reveal the answer!</strong></summary>

**Correct answer:** False.

**Rationale:**

The MLE is the parameter value that maximizes the likelihood. If

$$
\mathcal{L}_2(\theta;\mathbf{y})
=
c\,\mathcal{L}_1(\theta;\mathbf{y}),
$$

where $c>0$ does not depend on $\theta$, then $\mathcal{L}_2(\theta;\mathbf{y})$ is just a vertically rescaled version of $\mathcal{L}_1(\theta;\mathbf{y})$. The height of the likelihood changes, but the parameter value where the maximum occurs does not.

Mathematically, for any two candidate parameter values $\theta_a$ and $\theta_b$,

$$
\mathcal{L}_1(\theta_a;\mathbf{y})
>
\mathcal{L}_1(\theta_b;\mathbf{y})
$$

implies

$$
c\,\mathcal{L}_1(\theta_a;\mathbf{y})
>
c\,\mathcal{L}_1(\theta_b;\mathbf{y}),
$$

because $c>0$. Therefore, multiplying by $c$ preserves the ordering of likelihood values across parameter values.

Equivalently,

$$
\operatorname*{arg\,max}_{\theta}\mathcal{L}_2(\theta;\mathbf{y})
=
\operatorname*{arg\,max}_{\theta}c\,\mathcal{L}_1(\theta;\mathbf{y})
=
\operatorname*{arg\,max}_{\theta}\mathcal{L}_1(\theta;\mathbf{y}).
$$

This is also why analysts often work with the log-likelihood and drop additive constants that do not depend on the parameter. Taking logs gives

$$
\log\mathcal{L}_2(\theta;\mathbf{y})
=
\log c
+
\log\mathcal{L}_1(\theta;\mathbf{y}).
$$

The term $\log c$ shifts the log-likelihood up or down by a constant amount, but it does not change the value of $\theta$ that maximizes it.

So, multiplying the likelihood by a positive parameter-free constant changes the scale of the likelihood, not the location of the MLE.

</details>
::::
:::

::: {.Question}
::::{.Question-header}
Question 2.34
::::
::::{.Question-container}
**Open-ended Question**

A conversion experiment observes $Y\sim\operatorname{Binomial}(n,\pi)$, where $Y$ is the number of conversions among $n$ independent visitors. The PMF is

$$
p_Y(y;n,\pi)=\binom{n}{y}\pi^y(1-\pi)^{n-y},
\qquad y\in\{0,1,\ldots,n\}.
$$

Derive the MLE of $\pi$ from the Binomial likelihood.
::::
:::

::: {.Answer}
::::{.Answer-header}
Answer 2.34
::::
::::{.Answer-container}
<details>
<summary><strong>Click here to reveal the answer!</strong></summary>

**Answer:**

The likelihood is

$$
\mathcal{L}(\pi;y)
=
\binom{n}{y}\pi^y(1-\pi)^{n-y}.
$$

The log-likelihood is

$$
\ell(\pi;y)
=
\log\binom{n}{y}+y\log(\pi)+(n-y)\log(1-\pi).
$$

Differentiate with respect to $\pi$:

$$
\frac{\partial \ell(\pi;y)}{\partial \pi}
=
\frac{y}{\pi}-\frac{n-y}{1-\pi}.
$$

Set equal to zero:

$$
\frac{y}{\pi}=\frac{n-y}{1-\pi}.
$$

Cross-multiplying gives

$$
y(1-\pi)=\pi(n-y)
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
y= n\pi.
$$

Thus,

$$
\hat{\pi}_{\operatorname{MLE}}=\frac{Y}{n}.
$$

For the observed data, the estimate is $\hat{\pi}_{\operatorname{MLE,obs}}=y/n$.

</details>
::::
:::

::: {.Question}
::::{.Question-header}
Question 2.35
::::
::::{.Question-container}
**Multiple Choice**

Suppose a numerical optimizer returns the value of $\theta$ that minimizes the negative log-likelihood

$$
-\ell(\theta;\mathbf{y}).
$$

What has the optimizer found?

**A.** The least-squares estimate regardless of the probability model.

**B.** The maximum likelihood estimate, if the optimizer successfully found the global minimum.

**C.** The posterior mean.

**D.** The sampling distribution of $\hat{\theta}$.
::::
:::

::: {.Answer}
::::{.Answer-header}
Answer 2.35
::::
::::{.Answer-container}
<details>
<summary><strong>Click here to reveal the answer!</strong></summary>

**Correct answer:** B.

**Rationale:**

Maximizing the log-likelihood is equivalent to minimizing the negative log-likelihood. Therefore, a successful global minimization of $-\ell(\theta;\mathbf{y})$ returns the maximum likelihood estimate.

</details>
::::
:::

::: {.Question}
::::{.Question-header}
Question 2.36
::::
::::{.Question-container}
**True or False**

The likelihood function $\mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\theta};\mathbf{y})$ is constructed after the data are observed by treating $\mathbf{y}$ as fixed and the parameter vector $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ as the argument of the function.
::::
:::

::: {.Answer}
::::{.Answer-header}
Answer 2.36
::::
::::{.Answer-container}
<details>
<summary><strong>Click here to reveal the answer!</strong></summary>

**Correct answer:** True.

**Rationale:**

This is the core likelihood perspective. The probability model describes random data before observation. After observing $\mathbf{y}$, the likelihood views the observed data as fixed and studies how compatible different parameter values are with those data under the model.

</details>
::::
:::

### Goodness of Fit: Does the Fitted Probability Model Look Adequate?

::: {.Question}
::::{.Question-header}
Question 2.37
::::
::::{.Question-container}
**Multiple Choice**

A website analyst models the **number of page views per session** using a Poisson model:

$$
Y_i \sim \operatorname{Poisson}(\lambda),
$$

where $Y_i$ is the number of page views in session $i$. The Poisson distribution is a natural starting point for count data because it is defined on

$$
y=0,1,2,\ldots.
$$

However, the classical Poisson model also imposes an important mean-variance restriction:

$$
\mathbb{E}(Y_i)=\lambda
\qquad
\text{and}
\qquad
\operatorname{Var}(Y_i)=\lambda.
$$

This property is often called **equidispersion**: the mean and variance are equal under the model. Therefore, as a first descriptive goodness-of-fit check, an analyst can compare the observed sample mean and observed sample variance. In this study, the observed sample mean is

$$
\bar{y}=2.1,
$$

while the observed sample variance is

$$
s_y^2=9.8.
$$

Which conclusion is most appropriate as a first goodness-of-fit concern?

**A.** The Poisson model is automatically correct because the response is a count.

**B.** The variance is much larger than the mean, so overdispersion is a concern.

**C.** The response must be continuous because the variance is not an integer.

**D.** The sample mean and sample variance are irrelevant for checking a Poisson model.
::::
:::

::: {.Answer}
::::{.Answer-header}
Answer 2.37
::::
::::{.Answer-container}
<details>
<summary><strong>Click here to reveal the answer!</strong></summary>

**Correct answer:** B.

**Rationale:**

For a Poisson distribution,

$$
\mathbb{E}(Y)=\operatorname{Var}(Y)=\lambda.
$$

This means that the same parameter $\lambda$ controls both the centre and the spread of the distribution. If a fitted Poisson model has an estimated mean around

$$
\hat{\lambda}\approx \bar{y}=2.1,
$$

then the model also expects the variance to be roughly around

$$
2.1.
$$

However, the observed sample variance is

$$
s_y^2=9.8,
$$

which is much larger than the sample mean. The ratio

$$
\frac{s_y^2}{\bar{y}}
=
\frac{9.8}{2.1}
\approx 4.67
$$

suggests that the observed counts are far more variable than a simple Poisson model would expect.

This pattern is called **overdispersion**. It does not prove that the Poisson model is wrong in every possible sense, and it does not identify one unique replacement model by itself. However, it is a strong first goodness-of-fit warning that the Poisson equidispersion assumption may be too restrictive for these data.

In this applied setting, overdispersion could arise because some sessions are very short while others involve unusually heavy browsing, because users come from different behavioural groups, or because important predictors such as traffic source, device type, or user intent have not been included. In later count-regression chapters, this kind of pattern motivates alternatives such as Negative Binomial, Generalized Poisson, or Zero-inflated Poisson.

Therefore, the most appropriate answer is **B**.
</details>
::::
:::

::: {.Question}
::::{.Question-header}
Question 2.38
::::
::::{.Question-container}
**True or False**

A support team fits a Poisson model to **daily ticket counts** so that it can summarize the typical volume of incoming requests and plan staffing. After fitting the model, the analyst compares the observed ticket-count pattern with the counts expected under the fitted Poisson distribution.

> *True or false:* If the goodness-of-fit checks do not show a major departure from the fitted Poisson model, this means the Poisson model has been proven to be the true data-generating mechanism for daily ticket counts.
::::
:::

::: {.Answer}
::::{.Answer-header}
Answer 2.38
::::
::::{.Answer-container}
<details>
<summary><strong>Click here to reveal the answer!</strong></summary>

**Correct answer:** False.

**Rationale:**

Goodness of fit asks whether the fitted model is **adequate for the observed data and the modelling inquiry**. It does not prove that the fitted model is literally true.

In this support-ticket setting, a Poisson model might be useful if it captures the main pattern in daily ticket counts well enough for planning staffing levels. However, even if the observed counts look reasonably consistent with the fitted Poisson distribution, there may still be features the model does not capture, such as day-of-week patterns, holiday effects, product-release spikes, dependence across days, or differences between ordinary and high-volume support periods.

Thus, a goodness-of-fit check can provide evidence that a model is reasonable for a specific purpose, and it can also reveal signs of model inadequacy. But it cannot prove that the model is the true mechanism that generated the data. In applied work, the more realistic question is whether the model is useful and adequate enough for the decision being made.
</details>
::::
:::

::: {.Question}
::::{.Question-header}
Question 2.39
::::
::::{.Question-container}
**Open-ended Question**

A manufacturing team measures the **diameter error** $Y_i$ of machine-produced parts, where $Y_i=0$ means the part is exactly on target, positive values mean the part is too large, and negative values mean the part is too small. An analyst fits a Normal model,

$$
Y_i \sim \operatorname{Normal}(\mu,\sigma^2),
$$

and obtains the fitted values

$$
\hat{\mu}_{\operatorname{MLE,obs}}=0.02
\qquad
\text{and}
\qquad
\hat{\sigma}_{\operatorname{MLE,obs}}=0.10.
$$

The analyst then standardizes the observed errors using

$$
z_i
=
\frac{y_i-\hat{\mu}_{\operatorname{MLE,obs}}}{\hat{\sigma}_{\operatorname{MLE,obs}}}.
$$

@fig-standardized-diameter-errors shows an illustrative histogram of standardized errors from this fitted model.

```{r}
#| label: fig-standardized-diameter-errors
```
